questions.prompt_html:
Show that div (r n r) = (n + 3) rn.
You may use the following fact: grad ( rn ) = n r n-2 r
questions.prompt_text: Show that div (r n r ) = (n + 3) r n . You may use the following fact: grad ( r n ) = n r n-2 r
questions.solution_html:
Recall the identity div (φF) = grad(φ) ∙ F + φ div(F), where φ and F are smooth scalar and vector fields, respectively.
Substituting φ = rn and F = r = x i + y j + z k, we have
div (r n r) = grad(rn) ∙ r + rn div(r)
Now grad ( rn ) = n r n-2 r and div(r) = (x) + (y) + (z) = 3, hence we have
div (r n r) = n r n-2 r ∙ r + 3rn. But r ∙ r = | r |2 = r2, so it follows that  
div (r n r) = n r n-2 r2+ 3rn = n r n + 3rn = (n + 3) rn. Proof is complete.
questions.solution_text: Recall the identity div (φ F ) = grad (φ) ∙ F + φ div ( F ), where φ and F are smooth scalar and vector fields, respectively. But r ∙ r = | r | 2 = r 2 , so it follows that div (r n r ) = n r n-2 r 2 + 3r n = n r n + 3r n = (n + 3) r n . Proof is complete.

questions.prompt_text: Show that there does not exist a twice continuously differentiable vector field G such that curl G = x i + y j + z k .
questions.solution_html:
Assume on the contrary that such a vector field G exists.
Now div (curl G) = div( x i + y j + z k.) = (x) + (y) + (z) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 ≠ 0
Recall the identity div (curl G) = 0 for any twice continuously differentiable vector field G.
This contradiction proves our assertion.

y dx + x dy =  dA.
Therefore  y dx + x dy =  dA = Area A. (Proved)
